exercises.title: A: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.

items.sentence: For centuries, the depths of the ocean were regarded as presenting (1) ...... (MOUNT) challenges to any form of exploration...

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is cannot be overcome, so the correct answer is "insurmountable".

wrongOptions.1: The bare infinitive (1) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Active voice puts the wrong focus here — passive needs be + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice uses be + past participle — the focus is on the action, not the doer.

items.sentence: ...given that pressure, darkness and low temperatures made conditions far from (2) ...... (FAVOUR) for human divers.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not FAVOUR, so the correct answer is "favourable".

wrongOptions.2: The bare infinitive (2) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (2) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: The invention of the submersible prompted a complete (3) ...... (ASSESS) of what was scientifically achievable underwater.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is complete reassessment, so the correct answer is "reassessment".

wrongOptions.3: The bare infinitive (3) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (3) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Researchers have discovered creatures thriving in (4) ...... (SEEM) inhospitable environments on the ocean floor.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adverb from SEEM, so the correct answer is "seemingly".

wrongOptions.4: The bare infinitive (4) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (4) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: (5) ...... (PROBABLE) as it sounds, entire ecosystems flourish around hydrothermal vents that release scalding water...

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is improbable as it sounds, so the correct answer is "Improbable".

wrongOptions.5: The bare infinitive (5) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (5) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: ...in what can only be described as an underwater (6) ...... (WILD) untouched by sunlight.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is underwater wilderness, so the correct answer is "wilderness".

wrongOptions.6: The bare infinitive (6) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: Many shallow-water species are now (7) ...... (THREAT) as rising ocean temperatures disrupt their habitats...

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past participle THREAT, so the correct answer is "threatened".

wrongOptions.7: The bare infinitive (7) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (7) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: ...with coral reefs particularly close to (8) ...... (EXTINCT) in certain regions of the world.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is close to extinction, so the correct answer is "extinction".

wrongOptions.8: The bare infinitive (8) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (8) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Scientists warn that this type of (9) ...... (ECOLOGY) collapse could have devastating consequences for global food chains.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from ECOLOGY, so the correct answer is "ecological".

wrongOptions.9: The bare infinitive (9) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: The deep sea remains one of the most (10) ...... (MYSTERY) frontiers left on our planet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from MYSTERY, so the correct answer is "mysterious".

wrongOptions.10: The bare infinitive (10) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Superlatives compare one thing with all others in a group (the most / the -est).

exercises.title: B: Multiple Choice (Conditionals)
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct word or phrase.

items.sentenceBefore: We wouldn't have missed the train if you
items.sentenceAfter: taken so long to get ready.
items.originalSentence: We wouldn't have missed the train if you **haven't / hadn't** taken so long to get ready.
items.completedSentence: We wouldn't have missed the train if you hadn't taken so long to get ready.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "hadn't" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "hadn't" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "haven't" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.haven't: "haven't" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("taken so long to get ready.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: happen to see Clara at the conference, could you give her this message?
items.originalSentence: If you **would / should** happen to see Clara at the conference, could you give her this message?
items.completedSentence: If you should happen to see Clara at the conference, could you give her this message?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "should" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "should" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "would" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.would: "would" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("happen to see Clara at the conference, could you give h…").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Let's download the maps beforehand **in case / unless** we lose our internet connection.
items.completedSentence: Let's download the maps beforehand in case we lose our internet connection.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "in case" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "in case" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "unless" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.unless: "unless" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("we lose our internet connection.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: If you had double-checked the figures, you wouldn't
items.originalSentence: If you had double-checked the figures, you wouldn't **have / have had** to redo the entire report.
items.completedSentence: If you had double-checked the figures, you wouldn't have had to redo the entire report.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "have had" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "have had" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "have" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.have: "have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("to redo the entire report.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: born a century earlier, would Marie Curie have made the same discoveries?
items.originalSentence: If she **was / had been** born a century earlier, would Marie Curie have made the same discoveries?
items.completedSentence: If she had been born a century earlier, would Marie Curie have made the same discoveries?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "had been" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "had been" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "was" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.was: "was" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("born a century earlier, would Marie Curie have made the…").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: we leave by seven, we're going to hit the morning rush hour.
items.originalSentence: **Unless / Otherwise** we leave by seven, we're going to hit the morning rush hour.
items.completedSentence: Unless we leave by seven, we're going to hit the morning rush hour.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "Unless" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "Unless" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "Otherwise" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.Otherwise: "Otherwise" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("we leave by seven, we're going to hit the morning rush …").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: The performance would have been a disaster
items.originalSentence: The performance would have been a disaster **but for / if not** the lead actor's remarkable improvisation.
items.completedSentence: The performance would have been a disaster but for the lead actor's remarkable improvisation.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "but for" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "but for" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "if not" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.if not: "if not" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the lead actor's remarkable improvisation.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: We can reschedule the meeting for Friday,
items.originalSentence: We can reschedule the meeting for Friday, **supposing / providing** everyone is available that day.
items.completedSentence: We can reschedule the meeting for Friday, providing everyone is available that day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "providing" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "providing" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "supposing" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.supposing: "supposing" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("everyone is available that day.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: C: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
The only reason she didn't accept the promotion was the need to relocate. **for**
Had ...... , she would have accepted the promotion.
items.answer: it not been for the relocation
acceptedAnswers: it not been for the relocation

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is had it not been for, so the correct answer is "it not been for the relocation".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "it not been for the relocation", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Why do you think the project didn't succeed? **down**
What do you ...... to?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is attribute failure, so the correct answer is "put the failure down".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "put the failure down", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Clara felt unwell this morning so she stayed at home instead of going to work. **weather**
Clara was ...... this morning so she stayed at home.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is feeling unwell, so the correct answer is "under the weather".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "under the weather", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
If the manager reduced unnecessary spending, the team would trust him more. **to**
Were ...... spending, the team would trust him more.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is inverted conditional, so the correct answer is "the manager to reduce unnecessary".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "the manager to reduce unnecessary", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.

items.sentence:
The vet's advice was to avoid interfering and the injured bird would recover on its own. **nature**
The vet said that we should ...... and the bird would recover.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is don't interfere, so the correct answer is "let nature take its course".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "let nature take its course", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
If the research team can collaborate effectively, they should meet the deadline. **so**
The research team should meet the deadline, ...... collaborate effectively.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is condition, so the correct answer is "so long as they".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "so long as they", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I made a small error early on, so my entire presentation went wrong. **slipped**
My entire presentation would have gone well ...... early on.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is regret small error, so the correct answer is "if I hadn't slipped up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "if I hadn't slipped up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.

items.sentence:
There's a strong possibility that Laura will be selected for the scholarship. **being**
Laura's ...... for the scholarship are strong.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is likelihood of selection, so the correct answer is "chances of being picked".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "chances of being picked", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

exercises.title: D: Idioms / Collocations (Gap Fill)
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Throughout the conference, the organisers put all the necessary resources ...... the delegates' disposal.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is at someone's disposal, so the correct answer is "at".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: She managed to finish the marathon ...... dint of months of gruelling daily training.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is by dint of, so the correct answer is "by".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: ...... the risk of sounding impatient, could you tell us when the results will be announced?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is polite disclaimer, so the correct answer is "At".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: There's no clock in this room, but ...... a guess I'd say it's around three in the afternoon.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is rough estimate, so the correct answer is "as".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Marcus is a passionate sailor who goes out on the water ...... all weathers.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is in all weathers, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: I had completely forgotten my wallet, but ...... luck would have it there was a cash machine just round the corner.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is as luck would have it, so the correct answer is "as".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.

items.sentence: She rehearsed answers to every possible question so as not to leave anything ...... chance on the day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is leave nothing to chance, so the correct answer is "to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: My grandmother rarely gambles, but she does occasionally place a small amount ...... a horse race.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is bet on a race, so the correct answer is "on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentenceAfter: up several complications for the project team.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "thrown" — cause complications.

wrongOptions.A: "come" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "thrown" is correct.
wrongOptions.come: "come" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "thrown" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "got" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "thrown" is correct.
wrongOptions.got: "got" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "thrown" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "moved" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "thrown" is correct.
wrongOptions.moved: "moved" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "thrown" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

items.sentenceAfter: in the mountains now if we hadn't cancelled the trip at the last minute.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "could be lying" — hypothetical present.

wrongOptions.A: "might have lain" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.might have lain: "might have lain" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.B: "would lie" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.would lie: "would lie" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.D: "would have lain" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.would have lain: "would have lain" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When you see a finished past-time expression, reach for the past simple first.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "could be lying" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: She apologised for the comment, insisting that it hadn't been

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "deliberate" — not intentional.

wrongOptions.A: "instrumental" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "deliberate" is correct.
wrongOptions.instrumental: "instrumental" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "deliberate" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "purposeful" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "deliberate" is correct.
wrongOptions.purposeful: "purposeful" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "deliberate" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "desirable" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "deliberate" is correct.
wrongOptions.desirable: "desirable" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "deliberate" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "deliberate" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: The local team won the championship

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "against" — against expectations.

wrongOptions.A: "despite" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "against" is correct.
wrongOptions.despite: "despite" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "against" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "without" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "against" is correct.
wrongOptions.without: "without" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "against" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "below" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "against" is correct.
wrongOptions.below: "below" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "against" is correct.

items.sentenceBefore: Since they're planning a trip abroad, they're
items.sentenceAfter: back on dining out this month.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "cutting" — reduce spending.

wrongOptions.A: "dropping" does not express the present-perfect link to now that the sentence requires.
wrongOptions.dropping: "dropping" does not express the present-perfect link to now that the sentence requires.
wrongOptions.B: "moving" does not express the present-perfect link to now that the sentence requires.
wrongOptions.moving: "moving" does not express the present-perfect link to now that the sentence requires.
wrongOptions.C: "giving" does not express the present-perfect link to now that the sentence requires.
wrongOptions.giving: "giving" does not express the present-perfect link to now that the sentence requires.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

items.sentenceBefore: It would have been a perfect evening,
items.sentenceAfter: the power not gone out during the concert.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is inverted third conditional, so the correct answer is "had".

wrongOptions.B: "would" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.would: "would" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.C: "should" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.should: "should" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.D: "might" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.might: "might" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).

explanationContent.usefulTip: Name the conditional type first — that tells you which verb forms belong in each clause.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Third conditional: if + past perfect, would have + past participle — an unreal past situation and its past result.

items.sentenceBefore: No one wanted to break the bad news to Sarah, but I drew the short

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "straw" — unlucky person chosen.

wrongOptions.B: "stick" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "straw" is correct.
wrongOptions.stick: "stick" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "straw" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "pole" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "straw" is correct.
wrongOptions.pole: "pole" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "straw" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "rod" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "straw" is correct.
wrongOptions.rod: "rod" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "straw" is correct.

items.sentenceBefore: Authorities warned that the dam could collapse, so residents were

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "evacuated" — removed from danger.

wrongOptions.A: "escaped" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "evacuated" is correct.
